1:
The chapter opens on the very instant Solomon's prayer ends, and the witnesses gather around the fire that falls in answer. The reformed John Gill fixes the moment simply: no sooner had the king made an end of the prayer recorded in the preceding chapter than heaven replied. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the double sign — fire fell from heaven and devoured the burnt-offering and the thank-offerings, and the glory of the LORD filled the house, so that the priests could not enter; and the assembled congregation, seeing the fire and the glory descend, bowed with their faces to the pavement and worshipped.

Here the grammatical-historical witnesses close ranks against a critical suspicion. Because this fire from heaven is absent from the parallel at 1 Kings 8:54, modern criticism, following Thenius, had branded the Chronicler's account a later, unhistorical embellishment of the dedication. Keil and Delitzsch reject the charge, and Charles Ellicott joins them: there is no objective ground for supposing the Chronicler invented the facts; he must have found them in one of his sources, and it is gratuitous to fancy him more partial to miracle than the older writer — the fire of Leviticus 9:22-24 and of 1 Kings 18:38 shows the same God at work. Ellicott adds that the glory filling the house is a manifestation quite distinct from the fire, not a mere duplicate of 2 Chronicles 5:13-14.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the sign into a meditation. The fire came down not upon the killing of the sacrifice but upon the praying of the prayer, testifying God's acceptance as it had with Moses, Gideon, David, and Elijah; and to accept a burnt-sacrifice is, in the Hebrew phrase, to turn it to ashes, as in Psalms 20:3. That fire showed God at once glorious in himself — for our God is a consuming fire, terrible as on Sinai in Exodus 24:16-17 — and gracious to Israel, since it fastened on the sacrifice offered in their stead and consumed that. Henry presses the type home to the suffering of Christ, whose death was our life, and to the sanctifying Spirit who descends like fire upon the heart. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that the surest evidence of God's acceptance of our prayers is the descent of his holy fire upon us, and that the heart filled with holy awe of the divine glory is thereby owned as a living temple. So the witnesses converge: the fire is God's own Amen to the prayer just ended, judgment and mercy meeting over one altar.
2:
The glory that answered Solomon's prayer proves too great for the ministers of the house, and the witnesses read the priests' exclusion as the measure of the divine presence. The reformed John Gill pictures the scene concretely: the priests who had gone in to carry the ark were now unable to stand and minister, so that they came out and could not re-enter while the cloud possessed the house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the plain cause the verse itself supplies — they could not enter because the glory of the LORD had filled the LORD's house — and they treat the moment as the threshold of the great thank-offering and festival that the following verses recount, in which the Chronicler again lays his characteristic stress on the singing and playing of the Levites.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, raises the question that the repetition invites. Is this not the very statement already made at 2 Chronicles 5:14, that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud? He answers that the two notices are hardly different in wording — the cause is the same in both, and expressed in the same terms as at 1 Kings 8:11 — yet the writer plainly intends to record two distinct appearances of the divine glory, one before and one after the Prayer of Consecration, each attended by the same overwhelming effect upon the ministering priests. The glory came once as the ark was brought in, and now a second time as the prayer is sealed with fire.

The witnesses do not divide, and the point on which they settle is weighty. That the appointed priests, in their consecrated robes and about their lawful work, cannot so much as stand within the house is no failure of the ritual but its crown: the presence has come in such fulness that human ministry must for the moment fall silent before it. Gill fixes the fact, Keil and Delitzsch the cause, Ellicott the structure of the two theophanies; and together they let the verse say what the whole dedication has been driving toward — that the God for whom the house was built has taken possession of it, filling it so entirely that even those ordained to serve him can only stand back and adore. The witnesses hear behind the scene the older wonder of Exodus 40:35, where Moses himself could not enter the tent of meeting because the cloud abode upon it and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle; what happened once at the wilderness shrine is now repeated, on a greater scale, in the house Solomon has built. The exclusion of the priests is thus the surest proof that the prayer was heard and the house accepted.
3:
When the fire falls and the glory rests upon the house, the people are not scattered but drawn down in worship, and the witnesses attend to what Israel saw and how they answered. The reformed John Gill notes that the descent of the fire upon the sacrifice, consuming it, was all visible to the eye; and he wonders whether the glory the people beheld upon the house was a distinct brightness from the consuming fire — a bright, shining, glorious light resting over the sanctuary. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the movement of the cloud in the same way: it first came down upon the house and then entered into it, so that it was seen both within by the priests and without by the people.

On the posture of worship Wesley dwells tenderly. The people bowed with their faces to the ground, he says, expressing their awful dread of the divine Majesty, their cheerful submission to the divine authority, and the sense they had of their utter unworthiness to enter into his presence. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the same act into a lesson: when they saw the fire come down they did not run away affrighted but kept their ground in the courts of the LORD, and took occasion from it both to adore the glory of God with reverence and to acknowledge his goodness with thankfulness, crying, He is good, for his mercy endureth for ever. This song, Henry says, is never out of season; and even when God manifests himself as a consuming fire to sinners, his people can rejoice in him as their light, for it is of the LORD's mercies that they are not consumed, but the sacrifice in their stead.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the philology. Upon the pavement renders the Hebrew ritspah, the tesselated stone floor the Septuagint calls the lithostroton — the same word used of Pilate's Pavement at John 19:13. And praised means simply gave thanks, the infinitive standing for the finite verb; and the refrain, for he is good, for his mercy endureth for ever, echoes the standing liturgy of 1 Chronicles 16:34-41 and 2 Chronicles 5:13. Ellicott observes wryly that little in the whole section, save this last phrase, can be called characteristic of the Chronicler's own style — the words are the people's ancient confession, not the historian's. He records, too, the paraphrase of the Syriac and Arabic, which turn the refrain into a mutual exhortation — they said one to another, Give thanks unto the LORD — so that the praise passes from mouth to mouth through the kneeling congregation. The witnesses agree: the fire that might have terrified instead bows a nation in grateful praise.
4:
With the fire fallen and the people bowed, king and congregation together turn to sacrifice, and the witnesses weigh both the act and the exact language of it. The reformed John Gill passes over the verse quickly, joining it to the fuller account that follows. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in the mechanics of so vast an offering: the king and all the people presented the victims to the priests, who with the Levites slew them and sprinkled the blood; or perhaps the people themselves killed the animals, and, having caught the blood and collected the fat, presented these to the priests to be offered as the law required. The sheer number of beasts, he implies, demanded many hands.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording. Then is more exactly and, and the phrase rendered offered sacrifices is in the Hebrew were sacrificing a sacrifice — a durative construction, the Septuagint's sacrificing victims and the Vulgate's immolabant — picturing not a single act but a sustained, ongoing offering that filled the days of the feast. The grammar itself conveys abundance.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the spirit of the scene. The king and all the people offered sacrifices in abundance; with these they feasted the holy fire and bade it welcome to the altar. They had offered sacrifices before, but now they increased them — and Henry draws the lesson that the tokens of God's favour to us should enlarge our hearts in his service and make us abound therein more and more. He marks, too, the power of a good example: the king's zeal stirred up the people, for good work is likely to go on when the leaders of a people lead in it. The abundance was not extravagance but gratitude answering grace, a whole nation matching the fire from heaven with a fire of thank-offering from the earth. Most of these victims, the peace-offerings, were such as the offerers themselves feasted upon before the LORD, so that the sacrifice was at once a gift to God and a communal meal of rejoicing — the whole nation eating and drinking in the courts of the house it had just seen filled with glory. The witnesses do not divide: Clarke and Ellicott secure the manner and the grammar of the sacrifice, while Henry unfolds its heart — that a people newly assured of God's acceptance can find no fitter response than to give, and to give lavishly, out of the abundance he has first bestowed. The dedication, they agree, is becoming a festival of open-handed praise, led from the throne and taken up by the whole congregation.
5:
The Chronicler now names the staggering tally — twenty-two thousand oxen and a hundred and twenty thousand sheep — and the witnesses reckon both the numbers and their meaning. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the great thank-offering as the fitting close of the dedication: after fire had devoured the sacrifices and Solomon had praised God for the fulfilment of his word, king and people sealed the whole with this immense oblation, an account that agrees fully with the parallel at 1 Kings 8:62-63. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, secures the Hebrew — literally the sacrifice of the oxen twenty and two thousand — and notes that the older text specifies these as peace-offerings, words that seem to have fallen out here, though the numbers are identical in both accounts; where Kings says sons of Israel and house of Jehovah, the Chronicler writes the people and house of God.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke answers the natural astonishment at such figures. This vast total, he explains, is the amount of all the victims offered across the whole extended festival — the seven days of the feast of tabernacles and the seven days of the dedication together — not the slaughter of a single afternoon but the sum of a fortnight's worship. The number, rightly understood, is prodigious but not incredible.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the practical difficulty that so many victims created, and finds in it a further token of devotion. The sacrifices were so numerous that the altar could not contain them all; yet rather than any should be turned back, the flesh of the burnt-offerings and the fat of the peace-offerings were burnt in the midst of the court, which Solomon hallowed for the purpose — for in case of necessity the very pavement might serve as an altar. The blood of all, we may suppose, was duly sprinkled upon the brazen altar, but the sheer multitude of carcases had to be consumed on the hallowed ground of the court, so great was the offering. Henry's point looks forward to the next verse, but its root is here: a liberality so overflowing that it strains the appointed vessels of worship and must spill over onto consecrated ground. The witnesses converge on a single portrait. The numbers are real and enormous, gathered over many days; they tally, as Keil and Delitzsch stress, with the older record, and so carry the weight of ancient memory rather than of pious exaggeration; they mark the dedication as an act of national thanksgiving on the grandest scale; and they show a king and people who, having received the fire of heaven, answer with everything they have, until the house of God is dedicated not by word only but by the costliest and most abundant of offerings.
6:
The Chronicler pauses over the ministers who served that great sacrifice — priests at their posts, Levites at their instruments — and the witnesses read the verse as his characteristic care for the ordering of worship. The reformed John Gill glosses the priests' service plainly: they performed their offices, some in offering the sacrifices and others in blowing the trumpets, as the close of the verse makes clear. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the whole verse is the Chronicler's own addition, laid in after his usual fashion of stressing the ritual, and especially its choral and musical side, as at 2 Chronicles 5:11-13. Waited on their offices means literally that the priests stood at their wards or watches, at their appointed stations; the instruments of musick of the LORD are instruments of sacred music, made by David according to 1 Chronicles 23:5; and to praise means to give thanks.

The phrase that most engages the witnesses is when David praised by their ministry. Ellicott explains that the Septuagint took the words substantively, as if in David's psalmody, but that the Authorised Version gives the true sense: the Levites were David's ministers of praise, the instruments through whom his psalms still sounded. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in fewer words — David composed the psalms and hymns and appointed them to be sung by the Levites, with instrumental music joined to their voices, so that long after his death the praise was still, in a real sense, his.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws that thought into a doctrine of continuing worship. The singers and musicians played with the instruments David made and sang the hymn David had put into their hand; he had employed, directed, and encouraged them in the work of praising God, and therefore their performance was accepted as his act, and he is said to praise by their ministry. The dead king, Henry implies, still leads the living choir. So the witnesses unite in a single picture of ordered, inherited praise: the priests standing at their wards and sounding the trumpets, the Levites lifting the ancient refrain that his mercy endureth for ever, and behind them all the hand of David, whose devotion had shaped the very forms in which Israel now worshipped. Gill and Ellicott mark the closing clause, and all Israel stood — the whole congregation on their feet before the LORD while priests and Levites ministered, a posture of reverent attention that made the people not mere spectators of the worship but sharers in it. The Chronicler lingers here, the witnesses agree, because to him the music of the house is no ornament but part of the offering itself.
7:
The abundance of the offerings forces an irregular expedient — Solomon consecrates the middle of the court to receive what the bronze altar cannot hold — and the witnesses explain both the act and its warrant. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as a supplementary remark, its connecting particle marking not sequence but explanation, so that the sense is pluperfect: Solomon had hallowed the middle of the court because the brazen altar he had made was not able to receive the multitude of burnt-offerings, meat-offerings, and fat. Charles Ellicott confirms the point, noting the verse is slightly modified from 1 Kings 8:64: the altar which Solomon had made is that of 2 Chronicles 4:1, and the Chronicler's phrase was not able to receive simply explains the older text's was too little to receive. Both burnt offering and meat offering, he adds, stand in the singular in the Hebrew, each term gathering its whole class into one.

The reformed John Gill marks the distinction the passage draws between the dedication of the house and the dedication of the altar, and cannot resist a historical aside: the heathen, he notes, imitated such rites in the dedicating of their own altars — Pausanias records their use of ashes and water — and held feasts at the dedication of their temples, a custom that later passed to Christians, so that many of the old country feasts had their first origin in the dedication of a church to this or that saint. The observation is Gill's way of setting Israel's true dedication against its pagan and later shadows.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the devotional reading. Rather than any of the sacrifices should be turned back for want of room, the overflow was burnt in the midst of the court, which Solomon either hallowed for that service or hallowed by it — and Henry lays down the principle that in case of necessity the pavement itself might become an altar. The point on which the witnesses converge is that the very excess of Israel's worship, straining the appointed vessel beyond its capacity, called forth a sanctified enlargement rather than a curtailing of the offering. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott secure the grammar and the parallel; Gill sets the act against its heathen imitations; Henry draws the lesson that God will not despise an overflowing devotion that consecrates fresh ground to contain it. The bronze altar was too small for such a day, and so the whole court became, for that hour, holy to the LORD.
8:
The dedication broadens into a national feast, and the witnesses attend to its length, its throng, and above all its vast extent. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Solomon kept the feast seven days with all Israel, a very great congregation gathered from the entering in of Hamath in the far north to the river of Egypt in the south — the whole reach of the kingdom emptied into Jerusalem for the occasion. Charles Ellicott fixes the reference: the feast is the Feast of Tabernacles, kept its legal seven days as prescribed at Leviticus 23:34-36, the days counted from the fifteenth of the seventh month, as the parallel at 1 Kings 8:65 confirms; and the river is not a great stream but the torrent or wady of Egypt, the Septuagint's cheimarrous, the traditional southern boundary of the land. The older account, he adds, rounds the phrase with before the LORD our God, words the Syriac keeps, marking the whole vast assembly as gathered not merely to a city but into the presence of God.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, characteristically, hands on the Targum's rendering of the geography — from the entrance of Antioch to the Nile of Egypt — glossing the ancient landmarks by the names his readers would know. Between them the two schools fix the same fact: that the congregation which kept this feast represented the entire covenant people, drawn from border to border, so that no part of Israel stood outside the dedication of the house. The Targum's names, Antioch and the Nile, anachronistic as they are, rightly convey the vastness of the sweep — from the far northern gateway of the land to its southern desert stream.

The gathering from Hamath to the wady of Egypt is, in the witnesses' reading, more than a note of attendance. It answers to the ancient promise of the land's full extent, and it shows the dedication as an act of the whole nation and not of Jerusalem alone. The feast that follows the sacrifices is deliberately the appointed Feast of Tabernacles, the yearly memorial of Israel's dwelling in booths, now made the frame for the greatest thanksgiving in the nation's history; the seven days are the law's own measure, kept exactly. Keil and Delitzsch trace the throng, Ellicott the calendar and the boundary, Clarke the Targum's names — and together they set before us a united Israel, gathered from its farthest limits, keeping the feast of the LORD for seven full days around the house that his glory had just filled. The scale of the assembly matches the scale of the sacrifices: everything about this dedication is national, complete, and unstinted.
9:
On the eighth day the assembly holds a solemn closing convocation, and the witnesses labour to reconcile the two overlapping festivals the verse compresses. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets out the reckoning with care. The eighth day is the twenty-second of the seventh month, Ethanim or Tisri; the solemn assembly is the sacred convocation appointed at Leviticus 23:36, a detail the older narrative omits, though 1 Kings 8:66 records that on the eighth day Solomon dismissed the people after this final gathering. The seven days that preceded the Feast of Tabernacles — the eighth to the fourteenth of the month — had been kept as an extraordinary festival on account of the inauguration of the temple; and after this the Feast of Tabernacles ran its own seven days in due course.

This double reckoning, Ellicott shows, unlocks the obscure phrase of 1 Kings 8:65, which says the people kept the feast seven days and seven days, fourteen days — a compressed expression combining the two distinct celebrations, the dedication of the altar and the Feast of Tabernacles. He cites the Syriac, which spells it out: seven days of the feast, and seven days of the inauguration of the house; these and these, their amount was fourteen days; and on the day of the full moon in the month of Tisri the king sent the people away. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse the same way, distinguishing the seven days of the altar's dedication from the seven days of the feast.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry passes over the fine chronology to weigh the devotion it represents, marvelling that the whole congregation gave so many days together to the worship of God and the joy of his house. We must never grudge the time we spend in the worship of God and communion with him, he urges, nor think it long, nor grow weary of it. The witnesses do not divide: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch disentangle the two festivals and vindicate the fourteen days of the older account, while Henry draws from the same long feast the lesson that time given wholly to God is never time wasted. The solemn assembly of the eighth day, the atsereth of the law, was itself a day of holy convocation and rest, a fitting hinge between the extraordinary dedication and the appointed feast, on which the people paused to gather once more before they turned homeward. The eighth-day assembly, in their common reading, is the solemn seal set upon a fortnight in which a nation had done almost nothing but rejoice before the LORD.
10:
On the twenty-third day the great assembly at last disperses, and the witnesses read the people's homeward joy as the fruit of all that had gone before. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke tackles the chronological puzzle first: this twenty-third day is, he says, the ninth day of the temple's dedication, yet the parallel at 1 Kings 8:66 calls it the eighth. Following Jarchi, he reconciles them — Solomon gave the people liberty to return on the eighth day, and many did then depart, but he dismissed the remainder on the ninth, here reckoned the twenty-third when the fourteen days of the two feasts are counted in. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dates the day precisely, beginning with the evening of the twenty-second, and notes a small but characteristic touch: where Kings says the people blessed God for David his servant, the Chronicler adds the name of Solomon — for David, and to Solomon — just as elsewhere he links all Solomon's success to the promise made to his father. Some manuscripts, with the Syriac and Arabic, read for all the goodness, as in the older account, widening the people's thanksgiving to the whole sum of God's dealings.

Clarke lingers over the Targum's expansive paraphrase of the people's gladness, which unfolds the threefold goodness they blessed God for: for David his servant, on whose account the doors of the sanctuary were opened; for Solomon his son, because God had heard his prayer and the majesty of the LORD had rested upon the house; and for Israel his people, because God had favourably accepted their oblations, and the heavenly fire had descended and, burning on the altar, had devoured their sacrifices. The paraphrase gathers into one the whole drama of the chapter — prayer heard, glory resting, fire falling — as the very substance of the people's joy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the dispersal for its temper of heart: the people went to their tents glad and merry, satisfied to the full, having kept the feast without grudging the many days it cost them. We must never think the time long, he repeats, that we spend in communion with God. The witnesses converge on a single note. The assembly breaks up not in weariness but in gladness of heart for the goodness the LORD had shown to David, to Solomon, and to Israel his people; and that gladness, the Targum and the commentators agree, rests on nothing the people had done, but on the mercy that had opened the sanctuary, heard the king, and answered with fire. They go home rejoicing because God has been good.
11:
The narrative of the dedication is rounded off with a notice of completion — Solomon finished the house of the LORD and his own house, and prospered in all he undertook — and the witnesses read the summary as a quiet theology of success. The reformed John Gill simply marks that with these words the parallel history opens its account, joining the record here to 1 Kings 9:1. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the Chronicler's own phrase, all that came into Solomon's heart to make, which he takes as a paraphrase of all the desire — the word used at Isaiah 21:4 and 2 Chronicles 8:6 — of Solomon that he willed to do, the whole of his purpose for the house of the LORD and his own house — and he notes that this clause is wanting in the older narrative, the Chronicler's own addition. Keil and Delitzsch likewise read the verse as the seam that closes the story of the building before the divine answer that follows.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the lesson the bare notice contains. Solomon went on in his work and prosperously effected all he designed for the adorning both of God's house and his own; and Henry lays down the maxim that those who begin with the service of God are likely to go on successfully in their own affairs. It was Solomon's praise, he adds, that whatever he undertook he went through with — and it was by the grace of God that he prospered in it, so that the prosperity is traced not to the king's diligence alone but to the favour of the God he had honoured first.

The order of the two houses is not incidental in the witnesses' reading. Solomon finished the house of the LORD and the king's house — the temple named before the palace, the worship of God set before the comfort of the throne — and it is precisely this precedence that Henry makes the ground of the king's prosperity. The man who had begun his reign and his building with an act of devotion is now granted to complete all his designs. Gill and Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch fix the wording and the seam in the narrative; Henry hears in the summary the settled rule of God's dealing — that he who puts the house of God first will find his own affairs prospered by the same hand. The dedication ends, fittingly, on a note not of Solomon's achievement but of God's blessing upon it.
12:
The chapter's second great movement opens as the LORD appears to Solomon by night with a direct answer to his prayer, and the witnesses mark both the manner and the matter of the theophany. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke calls it a second manifestation, matching the appearance at 1 Kings 9:2-9, and notes the Targum's reverent phrasing, The Word of the LORD appeared to Solomon. The reformed John Gill observes that from here to the end of the chapter the account runs much as in the parallel, save that the Chronicler adds a specific answer to Solomon's petitions concerning famine and pestilence — a pledge, given as a specimen, that when the people humble themselves in prayer God will hear and forgive them.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the distinction between acceptance and answer. That God accepted Solomon's prayer had appeared already by the fire from heaven; but a prayer may be accepted and yet not answered in the letter of it, and therefore God appeared to him now, in the night, as once before at 2 Chronicles 1:7, to give a particular reply. And the heart of that reply, Henry says, is the promise to own this house as a house of sacrifice for Israel and a house of prayer for all people, according to Isaiah 56:7 — My name shall be there for ever, that is, there will I make myself known, and there will I be called upon.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott secures the text and its history. By night is implied in the older account, which adds that the LORD appeared as he had appeared unto him in Gibeon; and it is with the words I have heard thy prayer that the Chronicler's addition to the extant prayer begins — an added section which, Ellicott judges from its style, formed an integral part of the original text from which both Kings and Chronicles drew. The phrase an house of sacrifice, he notes, occurs nowhere else in the whole Old Testament. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, lay the two accounts side by side and find the order of thought agreeing, though the Chronicler here and there expounds a thought more fully or makes an expression clearer. The witnesses converge: the God who had answered with fire now answers with words, choosing this place for himself and binding his name to it for ever, so that the house becomes at once the appointed altar of Israel and the standing house of prayer — the pledge that the petitions offered here will not fall to the ground.
13:
The divine answer turns to the very calamities Solomon had named in his prayer — drought, locust, pestilence — and the witnesses read the catalogue as the covenant's own list of chastisements. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces each to its roots. If I shut up heaven that there be no rain recalls the threat of Deuteronomy 11:17 and Solomon's own petition at 2 Chronicles 6:26; the locusts here are the hagab, a winged and edible species named in Leviticus 11:22, where the earlier petition at 2 Chronicles 6:28 had named two other kinds, the arbeh and the hasil; and if I send pestilence answers the same verse of Solomon's prayer. The conditional particle, he adds, has the sense of whether — God ranging over the several forms his judgment might take. Each scourge stands already written in the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:15-24, so that in naming them God does but recite the sanctions his people had long known.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds only the Targum's solemn gloss on the last clause: the pestilence is the angel of death, the destroyer sent at God's command. The brevity is characteristic, but the note is weighty — behind the natural scourge stands a personal agent under the divine hand.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the three into a single doctrine of national judgment. God here supposes the calamities that may come upon a people — famine, and pestilence, and, under the figure of the devouring locusts, perhaps war itself, for enemies as greedy as locusts may be sent to lay all the land waste. The devouring locust, in Henry's hand, becomes a figure of the invading army, so that the three judgments reach heaven, earth, and the hostile nations alike. The point Henry presses is that these are no random misfortunes but the deliberate instruments of a God who governs nations, withholding rain, commanding the locust, sending the plague, precisely so that a sinning people may be brought to their knees. The witnesses converge on the same reading: the verse is not a gloomy catalogue of what might go wrong but a map of the ways God disciplines his covenant people, each judgment answering to a petition Solomon had already made, so that the king's prayer and the LORD's reply interlock. Ellicott fixes the covenant background and the exact species of locust, Clarke the personal terror of the plague, Henry the governing purpose behind them all — that the very heavens and fields and air are in God's hand to chasten, and that the chastening is aimed, as the next verse will show, at repentance and not at ruin.
14:
The most quoted verse of the chapter sets out the conditions on which a chastened people may find healing — if my people, which are called by my name, shall humble themselves, and pray, and seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways — and the witnesses expound each clause with care. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott anchors the phrases in the wider Scripture. My people, which are called by my name means, he explains, those dedicated to God, bearing his name as their own, in the sense of 2 Chronicles 6:33 and Jeremiah 14:9. Humble themselves echoes the covenant language of Leviticus 26:41; seek my face recalls Psalms 27:8; turn from their wicked ways speaks with the prophets, Hosea 6:1 and Jeremiah 25:5; and the promise to heal their land takes up the imagery of the psalms. The four conditions, in his reading, are not vague piety but the exact vocabulary of covenant return.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry unfolds the same conditions as a pastoral progression. God expects, he says, that his people who are called by his name, if they have dishonoured that name by their iniquity, should honour it by accepting the punishment of their iniquity: they must humble themselves under his hand, must pray for the removal of the judgment, must seek the face and favour of God — and yet, Henry insists, all this will not do unless they also turn from their wicked ways and return to the God from whom they have revolted. Repentance that stops short of reformation is not the repentance here required.

On the promise Henry is equally exact. Three national mercies are pledged in answer to the fourfold turning: that God will hear from heaven, that he will forgive their sin — the sin which brought the judgment upon them — and that he will heal their land, redressing all their grievances. And he marks the order as gospel: pardoning mercy makes way for healing mercy, as in Psalms 103:3 and Matthew 9:2, where the forgiveness of sin goes before the healing of the body. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott grounds every clause in the covenant and the prophets; Henry draws out the movement of the soul — from humbling, through prayer and seeking, to the decisive turning — and the answering movement of God, from hearing, through pardon, to the healing of the land. Together they present the verse as the standing charter of national repentance: the God who sends the drought and the locust waits, with hearing ear and open hand, for the first true turning of a people back to himself.
15:
God pledges his continual attention to the prayers offered in the house — now mine eyes shall be open, and mine ears attent unto the prayer that is made in this place — and the witnesses dwell on the tender condescension of the promise. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott links the words directly to Solomon's own petition, comparing them with 2 Chronicles 6:40, where the king had begged, now, my God, let, I beseech thee, thine eyes be open, and thine ears attent unto the prayer that is made in this place. The answer, in Ellicott's reading, takes up the petition almost word for word: what Solomon asked, God now grants, so that the promise is the exact echo of the prayer.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke hands on the Targum's reverent paraphrase of the anthropomorphism. Rather than speak baldly of God's eyes and ears, the Targumist renders it, it shall be pleasing before me, in the sight of my Word, that I should incline mine ear — softening the bodily figure into the language of divine good pleasure, and setting the mediating Word, the Memra, between the unseen God and the praying suppliant. The paraphrase guards the majesty of God even as it affirms his nearness.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the promise in its place within the divine answer: having supposed the national judgments of the previous verses, God here engages to answer the prayers of his people made in this place, so that the open eyes and the attentive ears become the standing pledge that repentance offered at the house will never be met with silence. It is the sinner's comfort, in Henry's reading, that the God who threatens the drought is the same God who binds himself to hear the cry that the drought wrings out.

The reciprocity the witnesses uncover is the heart of the verse. Solomon had prayed that God's eyes might be open toward the house day and night; and God replies not with a general assurance but with the very words of the request, binding his watchful regard to this one place. His eyes open and his ears attent are the language of unbroken, wakeful attention — not the fitful notice of a distracted king but the settled vigilance of a God who will not sleep over the prayers of his people. That the promise answers the petition so exactly teaches, in the witnesses' reading, how God delights to give his people the very thing they ask, and to give it in their own words. Ellicott fixes the echo of chapter six, Clarke the Targum's careful reverence; and between them they show the house of prayer established on a divine pledge of perpetual hearing, so that no prayer offered there, as the surrounding verses have promised, will go unregarded. The God who filled the house with fire now promises to fill it, age after age, with his listening presence.
16:
God declares his election of the house and the perpetual fixing of his name, eyes, and heart upon it — now have I chosen and sanctified this house, that my name may be there for ever — and the witnesses draw out the fulness of the pledge. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the words beside their parallels: have I chosen recalls the choice announced at 2 Chronicles 6:6 and again at 2 Chronicles 7:12, and sanctified this house answers the promise at 1 Kings 9:3, where the two accounts run closely parallel; perpetually, he notes, is in the Hebrew simply all the days. Keil and Delitzsch likewise read the verse as part of the divine promise the Chronicler shares with the older narrative.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fastens on the great clause my name shall be there for ever, and unpacks it in two senses: there will God make himself known, and there will he be called upon — the house is at once the place of his self-revelation and the appointed resort of his worshippers. The name, in the Hebrew idiom, is the LORD himself as he makes himself known; to lodge it in the house for ever is to promise an abiding, nameable presence, and not a passing visitation.

The witnesses, drawing on the verse's own weight, find in it a rising scale of divine commitment. God has chosen the house — the language of election, singling out this place from all others; he has sanctified it — setting it apart as holy, consecrated to himself; he has fixed his name there for ever — his revealed character and presence bound to it; and, as the verse closes, his eyes and his heart shall be there perpetually — his watchful attention and his very affection settled upon it without end. Election, consecration, self-revelation, watchfulness, love: the promise gathers them all. Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch secure the parallels and the plain sense of all the days; Henry hears in the name set there the double grace of a God both known and invoked in his house. The eyes mark his ceaseless watching, the heart his unfeigned love; together they promise that the LORD will neither overlook nor grow weary of the place he has chosen. Together they show that the temple is no mere monument but the chosen dwelling of the divine regard — though the verses to follow will make plain that this settled love does not cancel the conditions on which the people's own standing depends. For now, the note is pure gift: God has given the house his name, his eyes, and his heart.
17:
The promise turns from the house to the king, and lays down the condition on which his throne will stand — if thou wilt walk before me, as David thy father walked — and the witnesses weigh both the pattern and the terms. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the whole of the divine reply as setting before Solomon life and death together: God promised to perpetuate his kingdom on condition that he persevered in his duty, so that if the king hoped for the benefit of the covenant made with David he must imitate the example of David. The father's walk is made the measure of the son's security. God set before the king, Henry says, both the blessing and the curse, life and death together, so that the promise of an established throne came bound to a solemn alternative that the closing verses will spell out.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the wording against the parallel. Where the Chronicler says simply walk before me, the older account at 1 Kings 9:4 adds in integrity of heart, and in uprightness, a fuller phrase the Syriac and Arabic keep; and the following and do according to all that I have commanded thee rests on a Hebrew construction, and to do, which the Chronicler sometimes uses to continue a future verb, where Kings has the plainer infinitive. The differences are slight, but they show the Chronicler shaping the received words in his own idiom.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, take the verse as the opening of the conditional promise to Solomon, its thought agreeing with the older record even where the expression is made clearer. The condition itself is what binds the witnesses together. The throne is pledged, but pledged upon a walk — a life ordered before God as David's had been, obedient to his statutes and his judgments. It is not enough that Solomon build the house and receive the name of David's son; he must tread David's path. Henry presses the point home as gospel and warning at once: the covenant is sure, yet its benefit is entailed upon obedience, and the same word that promises the kingdom's continuance holds out, by implication, the loss of it should the king turn aside. Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch fix the language and its parallels; Henry fixes the moral. The verse offers Solomon everything his father was promised, on the single condition that he be, in his walk before God, everything his father was. The if stands at the threshold of the reign like a watchman.
18:
The condition of the previous verse is now matched with its promise — then will I stablish the throne of thy kingdom, as I have covenanted with David thy father, saying, There shall not fail thee a man to be ruler in Israel — and the witnesses attend to the covenant it renews and the ruin its neglect would bring. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the wording with his usual care. As I have covenanted with is in the Hebrew simply as I cut for — the word covenant being omitted, as at 2 Chronicles 5:10 — and the phrase ruler in Israel departs from the parallel's throne of Israel; the Chronicler, Ellicott suggests, has substituted a reminiscence of Micah 5:1, perhaps correcting a text in which the word throne had fallen out. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse plainly as the renewal to Solomon of the promise given to his house.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke will not let the promise be read apart from its history. There shall not fail thee a man — this promise, he says bluntly, was not fulfilled, because the condition was not fulfilled: they forsook God, and he cut them off, and the throne also. The pledge was real and the failure was theirs; the conditional if of the seventeenth verse was no empty form. History would prove the warning, not the promise, the thing that came to pass in the letter, for the sons of David lost the visible throne even while the deeper covenant held its course.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the promise within the same frame of covenant and condition, reading it as the assurance held out to Solomon should he walk as David walked. The witnesses converge on a sober double truth. On the one hand the verse renews to Solomon the very heart of the Davidic covenant — an unfailing succession, a man of David's line to rule in Israel — words that reach beyond Solomon toward the ruler foretold in Micah, whose goings forth were from of old. On the other hand the promise is entailed upon obedience, and Clarke's flat verdict stands over the centuries that followed: the line did fail from the throne, not because God's word broke, but because the kings broke the covenant. Ellicott secures the philology and the messianic echo, Keil and Delitzsch the renewal, Clarke the tragic sequel, Henry the moral frame. Together they show a promise both glorious and conditional — sure in the mouth of God, forfeited in the hands of men, and yet, in the ruler in Israel, pointing past every failing king to one whose throne would not fall.
19:
The promise darkens into a warning as God turns from the king to the nation and supposes their apostasy — but if ye turn away, and forsake my statutes and my commandments, and go and serve other gods — and the witnesses mark both the grammar and the pathos of the shift. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the but is more exactly and, and that the order of the Hebrew words implies a phrase has dropped from the text: the fuller reading, preserved in the older account at 1 Kings 9:6 and in the Syriac and Arabic, is and if ye turn away, ye and your children, the warning reaching beyond Solomon to the generations after him. Where the seventeenth verse had addressed the king alone in the singular, if thou wilt walk, the threat now falls upon the whole people in the plural, if ye turn away, widening from the throne to the nation it represents.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the supposition as a solemn realism at the very height of the dedication. God supposed it possible, Henry says, that though they had this temple built to his honour, yet they might be drawn aside to worship other gods; and he knew their proneness to backslide into that sin. The warning is not idle: it reckons with the deep bent of Israel's heart, even in the hour of its greatest devotion. The temple could not cure the heart that built it; the same people who now bowed before the fire might one day bow before the calf and the Baal.

The placing of the threat is itself instructive in the witnesses' reading. At the summit of blessing — the house filled with glory, the name fixed there for ever, the throne pledged — God will not leave the people to presume. The same voice that promised now warns; the conditional if that guarded the king's throne is set again over the nation's standing. Ellicott restores the fuller text, ye and your children, and marks the move from the singular king to the plural people; Henry lays bare the reason the warning was needed — the settled proneness of Israel to forsake the LORD for other gods. Together they show that the covenant held out in this chapter is no unconditional charm attaching to the mere possession of the temple. The house may stand splendid and the name may be lodged within it, yet if the people turn away and serve other gods, all the glory of the dedication will not shield them. The warning is the dark hinge on which the closing verses turn from blessing to judgment.
20:
The warning hardens into a threat of uprooting and desolation — then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land, and this house will I cast out of my sight — and the witnesses trace both the metaphor and the fearful history it foretold. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the Chronicler's language. Pluck them up by the roots refers, he says, to the children of the previous verse, and echoes the threat of Deuteronomy 29:27; the image is the reverse of planting a nation in its land, as at Jeremiah 24:6. He observes that where the older account had said bluntly, then will I cut off Israel from the face of the land, the Chronicler has somewhat softened the severity of the expression, though the substance of the threat — expulsion and the casting away of the house — is the same.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the verse through the lens of its fulfilment, and finds it terribly exact. How completely, he exclaims, has this been accomplished: not only have all the branches of the Jewish political tree been cut off, but the very roots have been plucked up, so that the day of the LORD's anger has left them neither root nor branch. The threat spoken at the dedication became, in Clarke's eye, the settled verdict of Israel's later history; what was uttered as a mere possibility in the night of blessing hardened, over the centuries, into the plainest fact of the nation's story.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the threat its full weight against the security the people felt. Though they had taken deep root, and taken root long, in this good land, yet God would pluck them up by the roots, extirpate the whole nation, and pluck them up as men pluck up weeds out of their garden, to be thrown to the dunghill. The witnesses converge on a single sobering point. The land was God's — my land, he calls it — held by Israel as a tenancy conditioned on obedience; and the house, for all its glory, could be cast out of my sight as surely as the people could be torn from the soil. Ellicott marks the Chronicler's softened phrasing and the covenant background, Clarke the precise and dreadful fulfilment, Henry the folly of a people presuming on a rootedness that sin would not let them keep. Together they show that the very chapter which fills the house with fire holds within it the warrant for its future ruin — the same God who chose the place reserving the right, upon apostasy, to cast it from his sight.
21:
The threat reaches the house itself, doomed to become a spectacle of horror to every passer-by — this house, which is high, shall be an astonishment to every one that passeth by it — and the witnesses divide their labour between the text and its terror. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott grapples with a hard reading. The Chronicler's this house, which is high is his correction of the older text's this house shall be high, a phrase meaningless in a context of judgment; yet the Syriac and Arabic read instead this house shall be laid waste, and the Targum of Kings combines both. Ellicott concludes that the original reading was neither high nor merely waste but this house shall become ruinous heaps — the Hebrew iyin, heaps, mistaken for elyon, high — comparing the like doom pronounced on Zion at Micah 3:12, a mistake he judges as old as the Septuagint. The passers-by, he adds, are pictured shaking the head and hissing in dismay.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the astonishment as the just amazement of onlookers at a fall so complete. The manner in which this disobedient people has been destroyed is truly astonishing, he says: no nation was ever so highly favoured, and none was ever so severely and signally punished. The greatness of the privilege measures the greatness of the ruin; a house once the glory of the earth becomes a byword, and the very completeness of the desolation is what arrests the passer-by.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the threatened ruin against a false confidence. This sanctuary would be no sanctuary to them, he warns, to protect them from the judgment of God as they vainly imagined when they said, The temple of the LORD are we, in the delusion rebuked at Jeremiah 7:4. The house that was high — high not only for the magnificence of its structure but for the holy ends it served — would come down so wonderfully as to become the astonishment of all the neighbours, its desolation echoing the lament of Lamentations 1:9. The witnesses converge on a grave irony. The very splendour of the temple, its height and glory, would make its ruin the more appalling; and the people who trusted in the building to save them would find that the building itself was not spared. Ellicott recovers the true reading of ruinous heaps, Clarke measures the punishment against the privilege, Henry exposes the folly of trusting the shrine while forsaking its God. Together they let the dedication's brightest ornament stand under the shadow of its possible overthrow.
22:
The chapter ends where judgment must always end, with the reason for it spoken aloud — because they forsook the LORD God of their fathers, and laid hold on other gods, therefore hath he brought all this evil upon them — and the witnesses read the closing sentence as the ruin explaining itself. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the wording: it shall be answered means simply that men shall say this, the passers-by giving voice to the cause of the desolation, on the pattern of Deuteronomy 29:24-25; and where the Chronicler writes therefore hath he brought all this evil, the older account names the subject outright, therefore hath Jehovah brought, a reading the Syriac and Arabic keep here as well. The judgment is self-interpreting: the very stones of the ruin will prompt the question, and the answer will be ready — they forsook the LORD.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws from the verse a whole theology of Israel's strength and weakness. While they cleaved to God, he observes, the most powerful enemy could make no impression upon them; but when they forsook him, then the weakest and most inconsiderable of their foes harassed, oppressed, and reduced them to bondage and misery. It was by no personal prowess, no genuine heroism, no superior military tactics that the Jews had ever resisted and overcome their enemies, but by the divine power alone; and destitute of that power they were even worse than other men. Their whole history, in Clarke's reading, is the commentary on this single verse: covenant faithfulness their only defence, apostasy their only real danger.

The witnesses close the chapter on a note that answers its opening. The dedication had begun with fire from heaven and glory filling the house, the visible tokens of a God who had bound his name to the place; it ends with the sober warning that the same name, forsaken, would draw down the very judgments the fire had seemed to promise against sin. Ellicott secures the self-explaining form of the sentence — the nations asking, the answer given — and Clarke the abiding lesson, that Israel stood or fell by her hold on God alone. Together they let the chapter rest on its great conditional: the house is chosen, the name is fixed, the throne is pledged, and yet all of it hangs upon a people's faithfulness, so that the last word of the dedication is not the glory of the sanctuary but the warning that forsaking the LORD would turn every blessing into its opposite. Grace and warning stand together to the end.
